We are requesting approval to deploy our AI-powered visual grading model into the production workflow for Atlantic salmon fillets. On a stratified holdout dataset of 12,450 fillet captures from the second production season, the model achieved 96.4% agreement with dual-inspector consensus labels on trimmed fillets and 94.1% on whole fillets. This performance reduces false passes by 78% compared to historical single-inspector checks. We designed the validation to reflect the rigorous quality assurance standards required for export certification. To ensure compliance-grade labeling, all training and validation annotations followed HACCP-mandated consensus protocols, where reference labels were assigned by agreement between two senior quality inspectors with specific adjudication rules for melanin spotting, gaping, and surface discoloration categories. This approach directly mirrors the facility’s most stringent manual audit process. We report performance separately for whole fillets and trimmed fillets because the export line applies different acceptance thresholds to those product forms, a necessity given that EU marketing standards permit differentiated grading criteria. Our validation metrics are therefore aligned with the actual operational decisions our system will support.

Our model development utilized image captures and defect annotations from this facility’s grading line over two production seasons. This focused data collection is consistent with EU Regulation (EC) No 707/2019, as our validation reflects the controlled conditions of a certified processing line under fixed imaging and trimming workflows. The holdout dataset was explicitly stratified across defect type, shift, and grade tier to ensure representative performance evaluation across all operational variables the model will encounter in production.

The system is engineered for integration into our existing HACCP workflows. If approved, deployment will begin with a two-week parallel run during morning shifts only, using real-time inference with human-in-the-loop auditing. This controlled introduction allows for performance validation under live conditions before a full rollout across all three shifts. In alignment with export compliance practice, we state that the system is validated only for Atlantic salmon under this facility’s imaging configuration. This is a responsible design boundary, consistent with internal clearance norms for QA-critical systems, and we make no claim for use on other species, other processors’ equipment layouts, or retail-pack imaging stations.

We propose to proceed with the deployment plan as outlined. We will implement weekly drift monitoring on key defect features like melanin spotting prevalence and surface texture, complemented by monthly performance re-evaluation against updated inspector consensus and quarterly audits aligned with the HACCP review cycle. **To:** Paris MoU Technical Working Group on Digital Risk Profiling  
**From:** The Port Risk Analytics Team  
**Subject:** Recommendation for Deployment of Enhanced Vessel Risk Prediction Model  

We are pleased to present our new predictive model for vessel boarding prioritization and recommend its approval to replace the current expanded risk profile (ERP) algorithm within the centralized Port State Control targeting system. On a temporal holdout of 14,300 vessel calls from 12 member states, the model delivers an 8.3 percentage-point improvement in precision within the top boarding decile. This corresponds to a 22% reduction in false positives among boarded vessels while maintaining 96% recall of vessels that would be detained. This performance gain directly addresses the core operational challenge of optimizing limited inspection resources across EU and EEA ports.

Our modeling approach is built on a foundation of operational relevance and regulatory alignment. We defined our training target using recorded deficiency counts and detention status from historical Port State Control outcomes. This ensures our risk labels are consistent with the official performance indicators mandated by Paris MoU reporting, creating a direct and transparent link between our predictions and the evidence-based inspection outcomes the convention requires. This design choice grounds the model in the same empirical data used for annual performance reviews and policy adjustments.

Methodologically, we constructed features to reflect the precise information available to port authorities during pre-arrival screening. We retained all historical inspection-selection fields—such as ship risk profile, flag performance tier, company performance, ship age, and prior deficiency history—as predictors. These inputs are already trusted and routinely consulted by PSC officers, ensuring our model’s outputs are compatible with existing decision workflows and can be integrated into current boarding justifications without creating new informational demands. All features were aggregated strictly at the vessel-call level using only information available before the inspection window opened at the destination port, which prevents any data leakage and accurately simulates real-world intelligence timelines. To evaluate operational utility, our performance reporting focuses on AUROC, precision at the top boarding decile, and calibration plots by port authority. The precision metric, in particular, reflects the resource-Xconstrained reality of inspection planning, where the quality of the highest-risk segment is paramount.

Our data processing adhered strictly to governance and consistency standards. Deficiency codes were harmonized across memorandum circular revisions by mapping older coding tables into the current deficiency taxonomy prior to model training. This alignment, required by Paris MoU Circular 2020/18, guarantees that multi-year training data meets regulatory standards for evidence-based and transparent system development. Furthermore, the dataset excluded any vessel calls with incomplete inspection narratives or missing IMO-linked vessel identifiers prior to feature assembly. This IMO validation step is non-negotiable for accurate vessel-level linkage across different port authorities and is a prerequisite for the full auditability demanded under Paris MoU data governance rules. Finally, we assessed model calibration separately for bulk carriers, tankers, container ships, and general cargo vessels. This ship-type stratification follows established PSC analytics practice, as inspection intensity and typical deficiency distributions—and therefore risk profiles—vary significantly across these categories.

We propose a phased deployment plan beginning with a six-month pilot in Rotterdam, Le Havre, and Gdansk. This will be followed by system-wide integration across all 27 member states, contingent on validation of real-world performance and full regulatory alignment. To safeguard ongoing performance, we will implement monthly audits tracking model calibration, flag-state equity, and detention detection rates. We will maintain a challenger model framework and conduct biannual retraining with updated inspection data. This model represents a significant step forward in precision targeting, and we request the Working Group’s approval to proceed with the pilot phase.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model's risk prediction is fundamentally confounded because both its labels and key features are downstream of the same selection mechanism, causing it to learn the prior inspection policy rather than latent vessel risk.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct a shadow pilot where a subset of vessels are boarded in random order regardless of risk score, and compare the incident detection rate of the new model against actual outcomes in this unbiased sample.

planted_issues.description: The team states that deficiency and detention outcomes are appropriate ground truth because they are recorded under a standardized inspection regime and therefore provide an objective measure of underlying vessel safety risk across ports. This justification is subtly wrong because while the data is official and standardized, these outcomes are themselves the result of selective, higher-intensity inspections for already-suspect vessels—making them non-representative of latent risk in the population and inducing selection bias. The model is trained to replicate prior selection, not predict unseen risk.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Understanding that Paris MoU risk profiling is a sequential intervention system where high-risk designation leads to more intensive inspection, which in turn generates more recorded deficiencies and detentions—thus outcomes are policy-affected rather than pure risk signals.

planted_issues.description: Inclusion of ship risk profile, flag performance tier, and company performance as predictive features—despite these being derived summaries of historical deficiency and detention outcomes used as labels—creates a feedback loop where the model learns to reinforce the same policy logic that generated the training data. The justification that these 'prevent any data leakage' is incorrect: while temporally pre-inspection, they encode outcome information from prior calls that contaminates the risk signal.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Knowledge that ship-level risk profiles in the Paris MoU are updated annually based on historical inspection outcomes (deficiencies and detentions), so using them as predictors of the same outcomes constitutes data leakage via derived fields.

ideal_critique: The use of historical deficiencies and detentions as labels assumes these outcomes reflect latent vessel risk, but they are actually influenced by the prior inspection policy, which selectively targets higher-risk vessels. This creates a circularity where the model learns to justify existing selection patterns rather than identify new risk.

Key features like ship risk profile and flag performance tier are derived from the same historical outcomes being predicted, creating a hidden feedback loop that invalidates the assumption of independence between features and labels.
ideal_defense: The team concedes that the labels represent observed outcomes rather than pure latent risk, but maintains that they are the best available proxy under regulatory constraints.

The defense contests that features such as ship age and prior deficiency history are legitimate pre-inspection signals and denies any temporal leakage, though it does not fully rebut the confounding effect of the compound signal from derived risk scores.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A shadow pilot with random boarding orders reveals the model fails to detect more critical deficiencies than random selection, indicating it is not discovering new risk but replicating prior selection bias.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The model maintains a high detection rate of critical deficiencies in a randomized boarding trial and outperforms random selection, confirming it captures meaningful latent risk beyond prior policy.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Detention prediction performance is inconsistent across port clusters or vessel types, with high performance only in high-surveillance ports.

must_not_claim: Excluding vessel calls with incomplete inspection narratives or missing IMO identifiers introduces selection bias and reduces generalizability
Assessing model calibration separately by vessel type suggests inappropriate stratification that inflates apparent performance
Harmonizing deficiency codes across circular revisions may introduce label leakage if mappings are not fitted solely on training data

ground_truth_rationale: The model is trained to predict outcomes (deficiencies/detentions) that are partially determined by the very intervention policy it is meant to inform. Because the features include derived risk scores (ship risk profile, flag tier) that were themselves computed from the outcome labels, the model learns a circular relationship rather than latent vessel risk. The ground truth is not disputed outcomes, but failure to generalize beyond existing selection bias.
difficulty_justification: This case is hard because the flaw is compound and subtle—neither the label choice nor the feature inclusion is individually invalid, but their combination creates hidden confounding. The addressed-but-incorrectly justification sounds competent and policy-aligned. Models will likely miss the combination (IDR failure) or cite decoys instead (IDP/IDJ failure). Skimming strongly suggests defense due to confident tone and strong metrics.

task_prompt:
**To:** Export Certification Review Committee  
**From:** Agricultural Vision Systems Team  
**Subject:** Recommendation to Deploy New Optical Grading Model for Citrus Export Compliance  

Our team has developed a new grading model that reduces grading discrepancies by 38% compared to the current in-line sorter baseline, achieving 94.5% alignment with final pallet-level QA dispositions. We recommend approval to replace the existing system’s default grading logic with our model, integrating it directly into the packhouse control systems to protect our export standing and long-term buyer contracts.

To provide actionable detail for the committee’s oversight, we report performance separately for premium-export acceptance, downgrade, and reject decisions. This tiered reporting ensures accountability across each decision class, as the operational impact of a misclassification varies significantly—mistaking a reject for premium fruit carries far greater phytosanitary risk than a downgrade-acceptance error. We report this granular view because our dispatch decisions and lot-level QA workflows are structured around these distinct categories, and the committee requires traceability at each level.

Our model development, hyperparameter selection, and held-out evaluation were conducted using image and inspection records from our Fresno packhouse across the 2022–2023 and 2023–2024 harvest seasons. This approach was a practical necessity, as each packhouse’s vision systems and proprietary QA programs operate in silos. Cross-facility data pooling for initial training is not feasible without extensive re-labeling against a unified standard. During preprocessing, all images were resized and color-normalized to match the inference interface of the deployed sorter. This step is required by the existing hardware to ensure compatibility with the conveyor’s latency constraints and the real-time sorting firmware; it is not a simplification but a necessity for direct integration.

The validation scope covered a temporal holdout of 1.2 million fruit units processed at the same Fresno facility during the 2023–2024 season. We treat this internal temporal holdout performance as representative of deployment readiness for other packing facilities that handle comparable fruit categories. This is justified by the observed stability of defect patterns across harvest cycles and, crucially, by our alignment with the *United States Standards for Grades of Florida Oranges and Grapefruit (USDA-AMS-FV-077)*. This standard defines the allowable defect thresholds and blemish classifications used in export certification. Our decision thresholds are directly mapped to these USDA criteria, ensuring regulatory traceability regardless of minor inter-facility workflow differences. Furthermore, our training labels combined initial machine-proposed defect classes with the final human QA dispositions recorded at pallet closeout. This is central to auditability: export certification depends on the final reconciled disposition, not a moment-in-time camera call, making the pallet closeout record the definitive ground truth under USDA compliance workflows.

We request the committee’s approval to proceed with a phased rollout. We will first pilot the model in one additional packhouse over the next two months, followed by staged integration across three more facilities aligned with the same high-value export programs. To ensure sustained performance, we propose a monthly audit tracking model-actual concordance per lot and a quarterly cross-facility validation using shared fruit categories, with calibration logs tied directly to camera maintenance schedules. This model represents a significant step toward standardizing our grading compliance and securing our export partnerships.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model validation is not sufficient to support broad deployment because it relies on internal temporal holdout from a single facility and assumes that alignment with USDA standards eliminates between-facility variability, which fails to account for hardware- and process-level confounders that affect image input distribution.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Shadow deployment of the model at two geographically distinct packhouses processing the same citrus variety under the same USDA grading rules, measuring performance degradation or calibration drift over two harvest cycles.

planted_issues.description: Model development, hyperparameter selection, and held-out evaluation were conducted using image and inspection records from one exporter-operated packhouse across two harvest seasons. This limitation is acknowledged in the memo but not recognized as a generalization flaw.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Generalization in ML systems deployed across physical environments requires testing under real-world variation in imaging setup, lighting, and workflow; internal temporal holdout does not substitute for cross-facility validation. This principle is supported by Zech et al. (2018), PLOS Medicine, which documents how site-specific technical features become confounding signals.

planted_issues.description: The methodology treats internal temporal holdout performance as representative of deployment readiness for additional packing facilities using comparable fruit categories. The memo justifies this by stating: 'We treat this internal temporal holdout performance as representative of deployment readiness for other packing facilities that handle comparable fruit categories. This is justified by the observed stability of defect patterns across harvest cycles and, crucially, by our alignment with the *United States Standards for Grades of Florida Oranges and Grapefruit (USDA-AMS-FV-077)*.' This reasoning is subtly incorrect because while USDA standards define defect thresholds, they do not standardize image acquisition or preprocessing workflows across packhouses.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Performance generalization in industrial ML systems depends on input distribution stability across sites, not just label standardization. Hardware variations (lens calibration, lighting, conveyor motion blur) and local QA override behaviors are known confounders. See Zech et al. (2018), PLOS Medicine, for the canonical example in medical imaging.

ideal_critique: The validation is limited to a single packhouse, which introduces risk that site-specific imaging artifacts (e.g., fixed lighting angles, lens aberrations, local QA override practices) have become part of the learned signal, confounding true defect detection.

The justification that USDA standard alignment ensures generalization is incorrect: standardized labels do not imply standardized image inputs. Covariate shift due to differences in hardware and process remains a significant threat.
Temporal holdout within a single facility does not validate cross-facility performance. The two are distinct validation objectives, and conflating them overstates readiness.
ideal_defense: It is reasonable to use internal temporal holdout for initial validation, given data-sharing limitations across proprietary packhouse systems.
The phased rollout plan with pilot deployment and cross-facility validation provides a mitigation path, though the lack of specified stopping thresholds weakens this assurance.
Color normalization and image resizing are necessary for hardware compatibility and do not necessarily degrade diagnostic performance if consistent across sites.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The model shows a >15% drop in precision for reject-class detection at the pilot packhouse compared to Fresno baseline, with evidence of local hardware artifacts (e.g., lighting flare patterns) being misclassified as defects.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The model maintains >90% cross-facility concordance with site-specific QA dispositions across three packhouses processing the same citrus varieties, with no systematic calibration drift observed.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Performance degrades modestly (<10%) at one pilot site but remains stable at others, with no clear link to hardware or process variables.

must_not_claim: Image resizing and color normalization introduce bias by discarding diagnostic visual features
Combining machine-proposed defect classes with final QA dispositions creates circular labeling (requires field-specific knowledge to dismiss)

ground_truth_rationale: The correct verdict is critique because the model claims broad deployment readiness based on single-site validation, and the justification—USDA standard alignment—fails to address how hardware and process differences across packhouses can introduce confounding covariate shifts. The flaw is compound: relying solely on one facility (ff_001_1) only becomes critical when combined with the assumption that internal holdout generalizes across sites (ff_001_2).
difficulty_justification: This case is hard because the flaw requires combining two facts from different paragraphs (IDR failure likely), the justification using USDA standards sounds plausible (IDJ failure), and the decoys (color normalization, label construction) are domain-specific and appear early. Pattern-matching models will likely flag the label construction as a flaw (FVC error) and miss the compound assumption violation. IDR will fail due to the non-obvious linkage between site homogeneity and generalization risk.
